replies.author: @Gehrich_
replies.text:
​@GameOn0827 "Good" without supporting reasoning is just a subjective judgment. "Garbage" is a hyperbolic way of saying it's not good, which is also a subjective judgment. Both review scores and player counts are appeals to popularity that result from a mix of subjective and objective judgments.

Objective aspects of a game would be those of actual quality. Is it well made, well designed, and well optimized? Is the art direction distinct, appealing/interesting, and clean enough to be easily readable and not cause headaches, dizziness, or other unintentional negative physical or mental states? Does it innovate? How does the total package compare to direct competitors?

Marathon is objectively well made. It functions well, has well designed environments, and it's optimization is apparently acceptable.

The art direction is where they first drop the ball. It's distinct and interesting in some aspects, particularly with the UI and presentation, but the assets are a common complaint. The one character that had any interest had her model butchered. The overly clean and reused environmental assets are readible, but they generate little interest and are only distinct due to how medical/corporate they are. There have been complaints of headaches, as well.

Lastly, does it innovate? Not really. It moreso just tweaks and rearranges aspects of the wider genre, which is normally fine in less competitive/tired spaces. How does it compare to direct competitors? Since the first server test it was apparent that it's just at or below par for its genre. It can please well enough, but if you're a fan of another game in the genre then there's little on offer to warrant switching besides some subjective preferences.


The game shouldn't have been made. Looking at successful boomer shooters new and old for inspiration in designing a full AAA revival of Marathon in earnest would have offered a fresh experience, filled a long-standing empty niche, and pleased old fans. It wasn't a smart business decision to forgo that obvious option.

author: @cobusvanderlinde6871
text:
There's this sneaky mischaracterisation in what they say gamers do.
I do not hope that the game will fail, what actually happens is:
1. I see concerning signs in the game'a marketting and messaging
2. I submit a prediction that it will fail (notice that I am merely describing what I expect will happen, I am not hoping anything)
3. The game launches and fails.
4. I keep my eyes on the launch to see how accurate my prediction was.
5. I redeem my prediction for a "told you so".

A "told you so" is a very sorry consolation prize and I would very much prefer to have a good game to play instead, but it IS better than nothing at all.

replies.author: @methodatx01
replies.text:
@Shade7x All games have some target audience in mind. And I am speaking about all games, which is why I did not speak about Marathon directly. Personal player reviews are great, but it is still most common for most people to check for information on games across review sites or Youtube. Players counts ALONG with player reviews though can project quite well a game's future lifespan. I'm glad that there are people who enjoy any particular game. But that does not make any company, product or service, immune to criticism and statistical data. It is up to the community that enjoys it to support it. It is not the responsibility of anyone to say they enjoy it just because someone made it. 

My most favorite game, Dekaron is one of these games. They came out with high numbers in 2007 and quickly fell due to insane microtransaction policies. They acknowledged the problem with their own policies and took that loss with dignity. What did they not do that kept them open to this day?....they did not blame the players or anyone else for their policies...this is why the backlash is happening. Want to say more but talking to the nursing home about releasing my dad, gonna end this here for the moment....
